meta.usage: Use patterns[].lineStart/lineEnd to read exact sections with read_file tool

patterns.title: 1. CheckUpkeep/PerformUpkeep Mismatch
patterns.rootCause: `checkUpkeep` returns data encoded in one format, but `performUpkeep` expects a different format. This causes ABI decode failures.

patterns.title: 2. Gas Limit Issues
patterns.rootCause: `performUpkeep` contains unbounded loops, multiple external calls, or complex operations that can exceed the registered gas limit.

patterns.title: 3. Unbounded Operations
patterns.rootCause: All logs in block must be processed

patterns.title: 4. Callback Safety
patterns.rootCause: Strict requirements can cause repeated failures

patterns.title: 5. Authentication Missing
patterns.rootCause: Functions source code contains hardcoded API endpoints without authentication, making them publicly callable and vulnerable to abuse.

patterns.title: 6. Response Validation
patterns.rootCause: Blindly trusts response

patterns.title: 1. Message Decoding Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Chainlink CCIP uses `uint64` for chain selectors, but protocols often use smaller types like `uint32`. Type mismatches during ABI encoding/decoding cause silent failures or reverts.

patterns.title: 2. Router Configuration Issues
patterns.rootCause: Router address stored as immutable or constant without update mechanism. When Chainlink upgrades router, protocol becomes incompatible.

patterns.title: 3. Extra Args Configuration
patterns.rootCause: `extraArgs` hardcoded in code rather than passed as parameters or stored in upgradeable storage. Future CCIP versions may require different formats.

patterns.title: 4. Source/Sender Validation
patterns.rootCause: `_ccipReceive` processes messages without validating the source chain selector or sender address, allowing malicious actors to send fake cross-chain messages.

patterns.title: 5. Callback/Receive Handling
patterns.rootCause: Complex logic, external calls, or state validations in `_ccipReceive` that can fail, causing permanent message loss.

patterns.title: 6. Gas Limit Configuration
patterns.rootCause: Default might not be enough

patterns.title: 1. Staleness Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Protocols call `latestRoundData()` but ignore the `updatedAt` return value, or use hardcoded heartbeat thresholds that don't match the actual feed's update frequency. Different Chainlink feeds have di

patterns.title: 2. L2 Sequencer Uptime Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: When the L2 sequencer is down, transactions are queued but not processed. Oracle prices stop updating but `latestRoundData()` still returns the last known price without any indication of staleness bey

patterns.title: 3. Circuit Breaker / Min-Max Price Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: When an asset's actual price drops below `minAnswer` or rises above `maxAnswer`, Chainlink returns the boundary value instead of the real price. Protocols that don't check for these boundary condition

patterns.title: 4. Deprecated API Usage
patterns.rootCause: `latestAnswer()` only returns the price without timestamps or round information, making it impossible to validate staleness or round completeness.

patterns.title: 5. Access Denial / Revert Handling
patterns.rootCause: Direct calls to `latestRoundData()` without try/catch. If Chainlink blocks access, the call reverts and the entire transaction fails.

patterns.title: 6. Decimal/Precision Handling
patterns.rootCause: Hardcoded assumption that all Chainlink feeds use 8 decimals. USD-denominated feeds use 8 decimals, but ETH-denominated feeds use 18 decimals.

patterns.title: 7. Phase ID / Round ID Handling
patterns.rootCause: Protocols that query historical rounds using `roundId - 1` will fail after a phase change because that round doesn't exist in the new phase.

patterns.title: 8. Price Manipulation / Front-Running
patterns.rootCause: Predictable oracle updates allow MEV bots to sandwich transactions or front-run price changes for profit.

patterns.title: 1. VRF Re-Roll / Manipulation Attacks
patterns.rootCause: When VRF request and fulfillment happen in quick succession without sufficient block confirmations, miners can reorganize the chain to produce different randomness outcomes.

patterns.title: 2. Subscription Drain Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Missing or inadequate access controls and rate limiting on functions that trigger VRF requests. Users can spam requests at minimal cost while protocol pays LINK fees.

patterns.title: 3. Callback Revert Issues
patterns.rootCause: Complex logic, external calls, or state validations in `fulfillRandomWords` that can fail under certain conditions.

patterns.title: 4. VRF Version Deprecation
patterns.rootCause: VRF V1 has security limitations and is no longer maintained. New security patches and improvements only apply to V2.

patterns.title: 5. Weak Randomness Sources
patterns.rootCause: Predictable randomness

patterns.title: 6. Request Configuration Issues
patterns.rootCause: Below Chainlink minimum (3)

patterns.title: Flash Loan Oracle Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: Protocols that derive prices from on-chain state (AMM reserves, vault share prices, LP token values) without adequate protection against flash loan manipulation are vulnerable. The core issue is a mis

subsections.title: Pattern 1: Spot Price Manipulation via Flash Loans
Pattern 4: Share Price / Exchange Rate Manipulation
Pattern 5: Faulty Oracle Configuration / Low-Cost Manipulation
Pattern 6: Precision Loss in Price Calculations (BalancerV2 Pattern)

patterns.title: 1. Staleness Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Protocols use `getPriceUnsafe()` or fail to enforce proper staleness checks with `getPriceNoOlderThan()`. The Pyth oracle returns the most recently stored price regardless of how old it is.

patterns.title: 2. Confidence Interval Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Protocols ignore the `price.conf` field or use arbitrary constant thresholds instead of ratio-based validation. The confidence interval should be checked relative to the price magnitude.

patterns.title: 3. Exponent Handling Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Protocols use raw `price.price` without applying the exponent scaling, or fail to handle edge cases where exponent values are outside expected ranges.

patterns.title: 4. Same-Transaction Price Manipulation
patterns.rootCause: The protocol doesn't enforce that the same price is used throughout a transaction, or allows users to update prices between operations. Unlike push oracles, Pyth prices can be updated by anyone at any

patterns.title: 5. Pull-Based Oracle Exploitation
patterns.rootCause: Anyone can call `updatePriceFeeds()` with any valid (signed) price that's newer than the current on-chain price. Attackers can strategically choose which prices to submit.

patterns.title: 6. Price Update Fee Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Protocols don't properly calculate the required fee, don't refund excess ETH, or don't provide adequate fee handling in payable functions.

patterns.title: 7. Self-Liquidation Attacks
patterns.rootCause: The ability to update prices on-demand allows attackers to create positions at one price and immediately liquidate at another, profiting from the price difference minus fees.

patterns.title: 8. Timestamp Validation Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Uses < instead of >=, logic is inverted

patterns.title: 9. Pyth Entropy (VRF) Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Same sequence can exist for different providers

patterns.title: 10. Integration & Configuration Vulnerabilities
patterns.rootCause: Cannot change endpoint if it moves
